title: Ethical Dilemmas in Small Island Communities

content.body: Practising pharmacy in a small Caribbean community presents ethical challenges that mainland practitioners rarely face. On an island where the pharmacy technician may attend the same church, lime at the same spots, and have children at the same school as their patients, the boundaries between professional and personal life become blurred. This section addresses the unique ethical tensions that arise when the pharmacy professional is also a neighbour, friend, or community member of their patients.
Pharmacy technicians in small island communities face unique ethical challenges that combine global pharmacy standards with local cultural, economic, and health realities.

In many Caribbean communities, cultural beliefs, language barriers, and traditional healing practices can influence patient trust and medication adherence. Pharmacy technicians must balance global best practices with local customs to avoid alienating patients or undermining health outcomes.  For example, in some islands, herbal remedies are used alongside prescribed drugs, and technicians must respect patient autonomy while ensuring safety.

content.text: 2. Limited Resources and Supply Chain Issues

content.body: Small island economies often have restricted access to medicines due to import dependency and high transport costs. Technicians may encounter situations where:

items: Medication shortages force difficult choices between dispensing available drugs and withholding prescriptions.
Overstocking is tempting to meet patient demand, but this can lead to waste, diversion, or noncompliance.

content.body: Patients may refuse prescribed medications due to side effects, cost, or cultural beliefs. Technicians must respect patient rights while explaining risks of noncompliance, especially in chronic disease management. In resource-limited settings, this can be ethically complex, as patients may rely on community support networks.

Caribbean pharmacies often face drug-seeking behavior from patients with chronic pain or mobility issues. Technicians must enforce prescription limits to prevent diversion, but also be sensitive to legitimate needs. This requires clear communication and documentation to protect both the patient and the pharmacy.

Technicians are generally not allowed to counsel on medications beyond label information. In small communities, where patients may seek advice on OTC or prescription drugs, technicians must avoid giving medical advice to prevent legal and professional consequences. This can be challenging when patients are ill and expect guidance.

With increasing international pharmacy partnerships in the Caribbean, technicians may interact with visitors or global health teams. Ensuring cultural competence is essential to avoid misunderstandings, build trust, and maintain ethical standards.
To navigate these dilemmas, technicians can apply:

items: Autonomy vs. beneficence (respecting patient choice vs. promoting health).
Justice (fair access to medicines in resource-limited settings).
Nonmaleficence (avoiding harm from overstocking, diversion, or miscommunication).
Cultural competence (integrating local values into practice).

content.body: In practice: In Trinidad and Tobago and other small islands, technicians should document all decisions, communicate clearly with prescribers, and seek guidance from supervisors or ethics committees when faced with complex cases. Building strong community relationships and cultural awareness can help resolve ethical dilemmas while maintaining professional integrity.

Confidentiality may only be breached in very limited circumstances, and typically only by or with the approval of the supervising pharmacist: (1) When required by law — court order, statutory reporting of certain communicable diseases, child abuse reporting obligations. (2) When there is an imminent threat to the patient or others — e.g., a patient threatens self-harm. (3) When the patient gives explicit written consent to share information with a named person. (4) For purposes directly related to the patient's ongoing healthcare — e.g., sharing medication information with a hospital if the patient is admitted. Even in these cases, disclose only the minimum information necessary.
content.title: The Only Exceptions to Confidentiality
